title: Configuration and environment

canonicalExplanation.summary: Configuration separates environment-specific values and policy from code, with precedence, defaults, validation, and secret handling defined for each source.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A service validates required database host and port at startup while reading the password from a secret channel excluded from Git. Your goal is to configure a project from an example environment file without committing credentials. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Ignore policy; Commits and history; Build and task commands. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Operational model
sections.body: Configuration separates environment-specific values and policy from code, with precedence, defaults, validation, and secret handling defined for each source. The learner should use this model to predict an observable result rather than merely repeat terminology.

sections.heading: Boundary and failure point
sections.body: Configuration can be executable or interpolated, environment variables leak in logs or child processes, and local, project, user, and system precedence varies by tool. A correct explanation therefore includes the applicable assumptions and avoids this common mistake: All configuration files belong in version control because configuration is not code and cannot contain sensitive data.

includes: Separate defaults, environment-specific configuration and secrets and document required variables safely.
Configure a project from an example environment file without committing credentials.
excludes: Configuration can be executable or interpolated, environment variables leak in logs or child processes, and local, project, user, and system precedence varies by tool.

outcomes: Configure a project from an example environment file without committing credentials.

examples.title: Configuration and environment: direct case
examples.situation: A service validates required database host and port at startup while reading the password from a secret channel excluded from Git.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the starting evidence: A service validates required database host and port at startup while reading the password from a secret channel excluded from Git.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.

Step 3 — Check the result against this correction: Version safe shared defaults and schemas, keep credentials out of the repository, document precedence, and validate effective configuration without printing secrets.
examples.result: The case supports the declared outcome because Version safe shared defaults and schemas, keep credentials out of the repository, document precedence, and validate effective configuration without printing secrets.

examples.title: Configuration and environment: boundary case
examples.situation: A surprising formatter setting is traced through project and user configuration precedence rather than changed randomly in source.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed context: A surprising formatter setting is traced through project and user configuration precedence rather than changed randomly in source.

Step 2 — Keep this boundary explicit: Configuration can be executable or interpolated, environment variables leak in logs or child processes, and local, project, user, and system precedence varies by tool.
Step 3 — Reject the documented misconception: All configuration files belong in version control because configuration is not code and cannot contain sensitive data.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Configure a project from an example environment file without committing credentials.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: All configuration files belong in version control because configuration is not code and cannot contain sensitive data.
misconceptions.correction: Version safe shared defaults and schemas, keep credentials out of the repository, document precedence, and validate effective configuration without printing secrets.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using the direct and boundary scenarios, explain why this belief fails: All configuration files belong in version control because configuration is not code and cannot contain sensitive data.

analogy.example: A recipe and packing line can model Configuration and environment: declared ingredients resolve to exact supplies, controlled steps produce an artifact, and labels preserve provenance.
usefulFor: Understanding dependency resolution, configuration, repeatable builds, and generated artifacts
limitations: Software supply chains include executable dependencies, caches, signatures, platform variance, and secret handling beyond a kitchen process.
